title: WordPiece: BERT's Subword Tokenization Algorithm Explained - Interactive | Michael Brenndoerfer | Michael Brenndoerfer
snippet:
## The WordPiece Training AlgorithmLink Copied

WordPiece training begins with a character-level vocabulary and builds up from there. The procedure has three stages: initialization, the merge loop, and final vocabulary construction. The overall structure is nearly identical to BPE; the critical difference is only in how merges are scored. [...] Notice how the algorithm naturally decomposes the word into morphologically meaningful parts: the prefix `un-`, the root `play`, and the past tense suffix `-ed`. This is not coincidental. The WordPiece training procedure, by using PMI-based scoring, tends to merge characters into units that are linguistically meaningful, because such units have strong statistical dependencies. The greedy tokenization then recovers these units efficiently.

Let us trace a second example to see the `[UNK]` fallback in action. Suppose our vocabulary does not contain the character `##z` (because the training corpus contained no words with `z` after the first character position). If we try to tokenize "puzzling", we would proceed: [...] As we covered in the BPE chapter, BPE chooses its merges greedily by frequency: whatever pair appears most often in the training corpus gets merged next. WordPiece takes a probabilistic perspective instead. Rather than asking "which pair appears most often?", it asks "which merge increases the likelihood of the training data the most?" These two questions sound similar but lead to vocabularies with different properties. WordPiece tends to prefer merges that capture meaningful morphological units, because those units appear together more often than you would expect from their individual frequencies alone.

title: WordPiece tokenization Â· Hugging Face
snippet:
## Training algorithm

âš ď¸Ź Google never open-sourced its implementation of the training algorithm of WordPiece, so what follows is our best guess based on the published literature. It may not be 100% accurate.

Like BPE, WordPiece starts from a small vocabulary including the special tokens used by the model and the initial alphabet. Since it identifies subwords by adding a prefix (like `##` for BERT), each word is initially split by adding that prefix to all the characters inside the word. So, for instance, `"word"` gets split like this:

`##`
`"word"`

Thus, the initial alphabet contains all the characters present at the beginning of a word and the characters present inside a word preceded by the WordPiece prefix. [...] ## Tokenization algorithm

Tokenization differs in WordPiece and BPE in that WordPiece only saves the final vocabulary, not the merge rules learned. Starting from the word to tokenize, WordPiece finds the longest subword that is in the vocabulary, then splits on it. For instance, if we use the vocabulary learned in the example above, for the word `"hugs"` the longest subword starting from the beginning that is inside the vocabulary is `"hug"`, so we split there and get `["hug", "##s"]`. We then continue with `"##s"`, which is in the vocabulary, so the tokenization of `"hugs"` is `["hug", "##s"]`.

`"hugs"`
`"hug"`
`["hug", "##s"]`
`"##s"`
`"hugs"`
`["hug", "##s"]` [...] Letâ€™s look at the same vocabulary we used in the BPE training example:

The splits here will be:

so the initial vocabulary will be `["b", "h", "p", "##g", "##n", "##s", "##u"]` (if we forget about special tokens for now). The most frequent pair is `("##u", "##g")` (present 20 times), but the individual frequency of `"##u"` is very high, so its score is not the highest (itâ€™s 1 / 36). All pairs with a `"##u"` actually have that same score (1 / 36), so the best score goes to the pair `("##g", "##s")` â€” the only one without a `"##u"` â€” at 1 / 20, and the first merge learned is `("##g", "##s") -> ("##gs")`.

`["b", "h", "p", "##g", "##n", "##s", "##u"]`
`("##u", "##g")`
`"##u"`
`"##u"`
`("##g", "##s")`
`"##u"`
`("##g", "##s") -> ("##gs")`
